notes: searches: ~13 (11 shared: 10 WebSearch + 1 WebFetch; 2 stragglers on GC-04-005). Local scholarship used at zero budget cost: Deanesly, The Lollard Bible (Cambridge UP 1920, full text) as the older academic pole; Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages vol. 1 (book-burning record); Wylie/Dowling as the 19th-century baseline White used. Anomaly check per charter §7.1: all six as-written rulings are accusatory (5 improbable, 1 discredited) while all six weakened rulings are well-supported. Re-checked before submission: the dossier deliberately groups six instances of one rhetorical pattern (a universal 'Rome withheld the Bible' absolute laid over a genuinely documented restrictive core), so the as-written/weakened split IS the finding, not drift; the framing-gap distribution (five 3s, one 4) carries it. The substance of the book's charge is vindicated at the weakened level in every single claim.
evidence_review: STATE OF THE QUESTION. The dossier's shared question is whether 'Rome withheld the Bible from the people' — medieval, early-modern, and (for three claims) present-tense 1911. The historiography has moved in two directions since White's sources (Wylie, D'Aubigné, Dowling), and both movements matter. (1) AGAINST the universal claim: no medieval pope or ecumenical council ever issued a universal prohibition of vernacular Scripture (Alister McGrath's formulation, echoed by Frans van Liere, An Introduction to the Medieval Bible, Cambridge UP 2014, ch. 7: 'no objections against their use, as long as these texts and their uses avoided association with heretical groups'). The restrictive decrees were regional and reactive to heresy: Toulouse 1229 canon 14 (lay possession of Scripture banned, psalter/breviary excepted, vernacular versions 'most strictly' prohibited — text in Deanesly, local file lines 3554-3586, from Mansi xxiii.197), Trier 1231, Tarragona 1233/34 (Jaime I ordered surrender of Romance Bibles for burning — Lea, local vol. 1), Béziers 1246, Oxford Constitutions 1408 (Wycliffite versions; unlicensed translations). Against these stand massive documented counterexamples: at least 18 complete printed German Bibles 1466-1522 (Mentelin 1466 onward, reprinted ~13 times by 1518) circulating with church toleration; the Malermi Italian Bible (1471) in dozens of printings; French, Dutch, Bohemian material; over 1,000 late-medieval vernacular Bible manuscripts analyzed in the revisionist literature (Andrew Gow, 'Challenging the Protestant Paradigm', and 'The Contested History of a Book', JHS 2009; Wim François and the Leuven school; Corbellini's 'Holy Writ and Lay Readers' project; Margriet Hoogvliet). Sermons, lectionaries, psalters, and biblical paraphrase were the laity's normal Scripture channel. Margaret Deanesly's Lollard Bible (Cambridge UP 1920, local) is the older academic pole closest to White's picture — she argued the Toulouse-type decrees were 'not merely regarded as exceptional local measures' (lines 3589-3591) and, via Erasmus, that 'many theologians even in Germany, where opinion was far more liberal towards vernacular Bibles than in England, denied the right of the laity to read the Bible' (lines 2023-2025); her generalization about pervasive hostility is the part the manuscript-evidence revisionists (Gow, François, Corbellini) have corrected. (2) FOR a real restrictive core, better documented than the revisionist summary sometimes admits: Gregory VII's letter to Vratislaus II of Bohemia (Hujusmodi salutationis, 2 Jan 1080) refuses Slavonic liturgy with the explicit rationale that Scripture is fittingly 'obscure in certain places, lest, if it were freely open to all, it would perhaps become worthless and be subject to scorn' — a documentary instance of deliberate obscurity defended in the name of reverence. Post-Trent the restriction became genuinely central and papal: Rule IV of the Tridentine Index (1564) conditioned lay vernacular Bible reading on written permission of bishop/inquisitor; Clement VIII's Observatio (1596) stripped bishops of that licensing power and ordered unauthorized vernacular Bibles destroyed; in Italy no vernacular Bible was printed between 1567 and 1773 (Gigliola Fragnito, La Bibbia al rogo, il Mulino 1997, from Holy Office archives; cf. her Church, Censorship and Culture in Early Modern Italy, Cambridge UP 2001); Spain's Inquisition maintained a comparable ban until 1782/83. Benedict XIV (1757) relaxed the rule to allow approved annotated versions. The 19th-century popes (Pius VII through Pius IX; Gregory XVI, Inter Praecipuas, 1844) condemned Bible-society vernacular Bibles read 'unguided', while restating the approved-version pathway. (3) THE 1911 STATE, decisive for the present-tense claims: Leo XIII's Officiorum ac Munerum (1897) codified the regime in force in 1911 — vernacular versions approved by the Holy See or published with annotations under episcopal care were licit for all the faithful without individual license; non-Catholic and Bible-society versions remained forbidden. Leo XIII's Providentissimus Deus (1893) promoted Scripture study; in December 1898 he attached an indulgence of 300 days (plenary monthly for daily practice) to fifteen minutes' Gospel reading; Pius X backed the Pia Società di San Girolamo, mass-distributing cheap Italian Gospels, wishing every Christian family accustomed to reading them. Simultaneously, lay Bible reading remained marginal in ordinary Catholic piety until Divino Afflante Spiritu (1943) and Vatican II — a point Catholic institutional history itself concedes (USCCB, 'Changes in Catholic Attitudes Toward Bible Readings'). (4) GC-04-005 rides the conciliar-censorship record: councils and popes demonstrably decreed the burning of books — Wycliffe's and Hus's works at Constance (1415), vernacular Scriptures at Toulouse/Tarragona, the Talmud from 1239, the Index's bans covering Protestant martyrologies (Foxe, Crespin), the 1596 destruction order (Lea vol. 1, local, lines 18677-18749; Fragnito) — but every located decree states doctrinal heresy or blasphemy as its object; no conciliar decree targeting 'records of Rome's cruelty' as such is known to the scholarship searched, and the genealogical node for that framing is GC itself with its 19th-century Protestant antecedents (Wylie; the 'no artifice, no exertion, no expense was spared... to efface all the records' topos). The scholarly commonplace that survives is White's adjacent sentence: medieval dissenters' history is known almost entirely through their persecutors' records. SOURCE GENEALOGY NOTE: Gow, François, den Hollander, and Hoogvliet form one revisionist network (shared Leuven/Brill venues) and are weighed as partially interdependent; Van Liere (Cambridge UP) and Fragnito (Italian archival school) are genealogically independent of that network and of each other; Deanesly is independent of all of them and leans the other way, which strengthens the points on which all converge: real, regional, reactive restriction; no universal standing ban; a genuinely severe centralized regime only in the post-Tridentine Italian/Spanish orbit; active papal promotion of approved-version reading by 1898-1911.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет шесть утверждений книги о том, что Рим прятал Библию от народа, от средневековья до настоящего времени 1911 года. В авторских формулировках ни одно не устояло: единой всеобщей политики сокрытия не существовало, до Лютера в Германии напечатали не менее 18 полных Библий, а к 1911 году папы сами поощряли чтение одобренных переводов, вплоть до индульгенции за ежедневное чтение Евангелия (1898). При этом ослабленные версии всех шести утверждений подтверждаются: Рим веками ограничивал доступ мирян к Писанию на народном языке, после Тридентского собора в Италии и Испании действовал почти полный запрет, документированный по архивам инквизиции (Fragnito), а самостоятельное толкование Писания церковь официально запрещала. Главный итог досье и есть этот разрыв: абсолютная риторика ложна, ограничительное ядро документировано. Картину изменил бы найденный соборный декрет общецерковного действия о сокрытии Писания или доказательства, что разрешения 1897-1911 годов оставались мертвой буквой по всей церкви.

quantifier_audit: It had been Rome's policy (standing, institution-wide)
locked up in an unknown tongue

components.component: The Church's authoritative Scripture was Latin, and church authorities at documented times and places restricted or forbade lay vernacular access (Gregory VII 1080; Toulouse 1229; Tarragona 1233/34; Oxford 1408; Rule IV 1564; Clementine Observatio 1596; Italy 1567-1773).
components.rationale: Every named instrument is documented in the evidence review; even the revisionists (Van Liere, Gow, François) affirm real restriction where heresy threatened, and Fragnito documents a centralized post-Tridentine ban from the Holy Office archives.

components.component: This restriction constituted Rome's single standing policy across the era — the Bible kept 'locked up' and 'hidden away from the people' as such.
components.rationale: No universal prohibition was ever issued by pope or ecumenical council (McGrath's formulation; Van Liere, Cambridge UP 2014). Multiple well-provenanced counterexamples defeat the standing-policy universal: 18 complete printed German Bibles 1466-1522 circulating with church toleration, the Malermi Italian Bible (1471, dozens of printings), 1,000+ late-medieval vernacular Bible manuscripts (Gow, Corbellini, Hoogvliet). Restriction was regional, reactive, and period-specific until Trent, and even the Tridentine regime was a license system, not concealment as such. Not discredited: a substantial documented core (including the 1567-1773 Italian ban) keeps the characterization partially true for major regions and periods.

components.component: The policy operated 'under a profession of reverence for the Bible'.
components.rationale: As description (not as hypocrisy verdict) this matches the documents: Gregory VII's 1080 letter defends obscurity precisely to protect Scripture from being 'held cheap'; Rule IV frames restriction as protecting Scripture from temerity. The Church professed reverence while restricting; the insinuation of bad faith goes beyond the evidence but the descriptive frame stands.

components.component: Rome intended to keep Scripture from the people (concealment as aim).
components.rationale: For the restrictive episodes the intent is documentary, not inferred: Toulouse canon 14 'most strictly' prohibits lay vernacular possession; Gregory VII states the obscurity rationale in his own words; the 1596 Observatio orders destruction. As a universal standing intent it fails with component 2, but the scope failure is carried there; the motive itself, where restriction occurred, is documented.

rulings.weakened_version: Rome kept its authoritative Bible in Latin and at many times and places — including by centralized post-Tridentine legislation in Italy and Spain — restricted or forbade lay access to vernacular Scripture, so that for centuries most common people could not read the Bible for themselves.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest published case for the as-written universal is the older academic pole: Deanesly (Lollard Bible, Cambridge UP 1920) argued the Toulouse-type decrees were 'not merely regarded as exceptional local measures' and marshaled Erasmus's testimony that even liberal Germany's theologians widely denied lay Bible reading; Fragnito's archival work adds a genuinely centralized papal ban for Italy 1567-1773. This case fails for the claim as written on evidence, not confession: the manuscript and print record assembled since Deanesly (Gow's JHS 2009 study of German Bibles; the 1,000+ vernacular manuscripts of the Corbellini project; 18 pre-Luther printed German Bibles never suppressed) shows tolerated, abundant vernacular Scripture across large regions and periods, which a standing universal concealment policy cannot accommodate; Fragnito's ban is real but bounded (post-1559, Italian/Spanish orbit) and therefore proves the weakened version rather than the universal. No Phase-1 defense object exists for this claim (disputed skipped the defender); the verifier's Deanesly-based nuance is confirmed and extended with post-1920 scholarship.

rulings.would_change_this: A documented papal or conciliar instrument of universal, church-wide scope mandating concealment of Scripture before Trent, or archival evidence that the pre-Luther German and Italian printed Bibles circulated only against active central suppression, would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open or probable.
rulings.notes: Ch. 15 context (French Revolution chapter) makes this a past-tense characterization of the papal era, so the 1911 relaxations are not counterexamples here; the medieval/early-modern record alone decides it.

quantifier_audit: Rome withheld the Bible from the people (unrestricted 'the people')
restore to men the word of God (presupposes prior withholding)

components.component: Rome withheld the Bible from the people (universal withholding).
components.rationale: Same evidence base as GC-15-028 component 2: no universal prohibition ever issued; abundant tolerated vernacular Scripture (18 pre-Luther printed German Bibles; Malermi 1471; 1,000+ manuscripts) defeats the universal, while the documented regional/post-Tridentine restrictions keep the kernel alive.

components.component: Rome 'required all men to accept her teachings in its place' — church teaching as substitute for Scripture, imposed on all.
components.rationale: The documented reality is mediation, not substitution: Trent Sess. IV reserved authoritative interpretation to the Church and bound the faithful to its sense of Scripture, and laity received doctrine through preaching and catechesis; but the Church presented its teaching as the authentic meaning of Scripture, not a replacement for it, and 'all men' overreaches even the Church's own jurisdiction claims as practiced. The substitution frame with the universal quantifier fails; the mediation core is well documented.

components.component: The Reformation's work restored/expanded direct access to the word of God.
components.rationale: Even the revisionists who defeat the 'withheld' absolute affirm that the Reformation transformed the scale and centrality of lay vernacular Bible reading; Gow's own paradigm critique concerns the pre-history, not the Reformation expansion itself. The verifier's Deanesly-based finding stands.

rulings.weakened_version: Rome restricted lay access to vernacular Scripture and required the faithful to receive doctrine through the Church's authoritative teaching and interpretation; the Reformation dramatically expanded direct lay access to the Bible.
rulings.minority_engagement: Best case for the as-written absolutes: Deanesly's reading of the decrees as more than exceptional local measures, plus Fragnito's post-Tridentine Italian ban, plus the documented requirement of assent to magisterial interpretation (Trent Sess. IV). It fails as written because the counter-record (tolerated printed and manuscript vernacular Bibles across Germany, Italy pre-1559, the Low Countries and France) is incompatible with universal withholding, and because 'in its place' misdescribes the Church's own documented self-understanding (teaching as the authentic sense of Scripture). The 19th-century historiography behind White (Wylie vol. 1, local, 'The Bible began to be hidden from the people') asserted exactly this claim, which situates it as period-standard rhetoric rather than fabrication. No Phase-1 defense object exists (ch. 21 defender pass-through, notes field); the verifier's disputed rationale is confirmed and sharpened to a dual ruling.

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism vol. 1 — local file (period source used by White)
sources.independence_note: 19th-century Protestant historiography; establishes the genealogy of White's framing, not its truth.

rulings.would_change_this: Evidence that the pre-Reformation vernacular Bible corpus was numerically marginal or actively suppressed church-wide (a quantitative codicological study reversing Gow/Corbellini), or a documented universal decree, would raise the as-written ruling; evidence that Reformation-era access gains were illusory would lower the third component.

quantifier_audit: select... interpret to suit themselves (ethnographic present)
deny them the privilege of studying the Bible

components.component: Papal leaders select portions of Scripture and interpret them self-servingly ('as best serve their purpose', 'to suit themselves').
components.rationale: Motive attribution of institutional bad faith. No documentary evidence of self-serving intent as a general characterization; the competing mainstream account of the same behavior — selection via lectionary and catechesis, interpretation bound to patristic consensus and magisterial rule — has direct documentary support (Trent Sess. IV's own stated rationale; catechisms). Per charter §3, undocumented motive with a documented rival account caps at improbable.

components.component: Papal leaders deny the people the privilege of studying the Bible (present tense, read against the post-Tridentine and 1911 state).
components.rationale: As a universal present-tense fact it fails on the 1911 state the paragraph inhabits: Officiorum ac Munerum (1897) made approved annotated vernacular versions licit for all the faithful; Leo XIII indulgenced daily Gospel reading (1898); Pius X promoted mass Gospel distribution. Historically the denial was real but regional and period-specific (Toulouse 1229 through the 1596-1757 Italian/Spanish ban). A real documented core, a failed universal.

components.component: Papal leaders deny the people 'understanding its sacred truths for themselves' — i.e., the right of private judgment/interpretation.
components.rationale: The Phase-1 defender's central point, adopted: Trent Sess. IV (1546) and Vatican I (Dei Filius, 1870) reserved authentic interpretation to the Church and forbade interpretation against the Church's sense and the unanimous consent of the Fathers; the anti-modernist decrees (1907) reinforced it. Denial of private interpretation in the Protestant sense was settled, documented Catholic doctrine in 1911.

rulings.weakened_version: Papal authorities presented Scripture to the laity selectively (through lectionary and catechesis), reserved authoritative interpretation to the Church while denying the right of private interpretation, and historically restricted independent lay Bible study.
rulings.minority_engagement: The Phase-1 defender (verdict_downgraded, anachronistic -> disputed) is answered point by point. (1) Its Deanesly/Erasmus finding — that even in liberal Germany many theologians denied lay Bible reading — is accepted and feeds the weakened ruling; it documents widespread clerical opposition, not a universal institutional denial. (2) Its private-judgment reading of 'understanding for themselves' is accepted in full: component 3 rules well-supported on Trent/Vatican I. (3) Its ethnographic-present argument, however, cuts against the defense on the access clause: if the paragraph describes the papal system as of 1911, the governing law was the 1897 constitution plus the 1898 indulgence and Pius X's Gospel campaign, under which 'deny them the privilege of studying the Bible' fails as written — as the defender itself conceded ('reads more sweepingly than the record cleanly bears'). The motive clause fails separately under the §3 cap. The strongest external defense (Protestant-polemical tradition: interpretation-monopoly equals denial of study) conflates interpretation with access; the documents distinguish them.

sources.independence_note: Primary doctrinal texts read through the standard scholarly account of magisterial interpretive authority.

sources.independence_note: The codified 1911-era law on vernacular versions; independent of the doctrinal-interpretation documents.

sources.independence_note: Independent modern synthesis for the medieval-access component.

sources.ref: Leo XIII 1898 Gospel-reading indulgence; Pius X and the Pia Società di San Girolamo (documented via Providentissimus Deus context and Catholic institutional records)
sources.independence_note: Papal acts independent of the Index legislation line; both defeat the present-tense universal.

rulings.would_change_this: Curial correspondence or internal documents evidencing deliberate self-serving selection of Scripture would raise the motive component; diocesan-level evidence that the 1897-1911 approved-version permissions were systematically refused church-wide would raise the access component back toward the claim.
rulings.notes: Only claim in the dossier with a Phase-1 defense object; the defense is incorporated (component 3) and answered (components 1-2).

quantifier_audit: They are not allowed access to His word (categorical negation of permission)
therefore they do not discern the truth

components.component: Catholic laity in 1911 are not allowed access to the Bible.
components.rationale: A statement of norm, and the documented norm said the opposite by 1911: Officiorum ac Munerum (1897) made Holy-See-approved or episcopally supervised annotated vernacular versions licit for all the faithful; Leo XIII attached an indulgence (Dec 1898) to fifteen minutes' daily Gospel reading; Providentissimus Deus (1893) urged Scripture study; Pius X's Society of St. Jerome mass-distributed Italian Gospels with his explicit wish that every family read them. Multiple independent, undisputed documentary counterexamples to the categorical negation — the §6.3 threshold for discredited is met. What remained forbidden was non-Catholic/unannotated versions, which is a conditioned-access regime, not 'not allowed access'.

components.component: 'Therefore they do not discern the truth' — the causal-theological consequence that lacking Scripture, Catholics fail to discern (White's doctrinal) truth.
components.rationale: Out of scope per §9: 'the truth' here is the book's own doctrinal system; whether Bible access leads to it is a theological premise this adjudication does not evaluate. The component is conditional on that premise and receives no ruling.

rulings.weakened_version: Catholic laity were long restricted in direct Bible access and even in 1911 could licitly use only church-approved annotated versions, with unmediated Bible reading playing a marginal role in ordinary Catholic lay piety.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the as-written claim is the de facto argument of the era's Protestant observers (and of White's defenders): approved versions were expensive and scarce in Catholic heartlands, clergy in Latin countries confiscated and sometimes burned colporteurs' Bibles, and ordinary Catholics in 1911 in practice had no Scripture in hand — a picture partially conceded by Catholic institutional history itself (USCCB: lay Bible reading identified as 'Protestant' until the mid-20th century). The argument is real and carries the weakened version to well-supported, but it cannot save the as-written wording: 'not allowed' asserts a norm, the confiscations targeted forbidden (Protestant) versions specifically, and the norm from 1897-1911 demonstrably allowed and rewarded reading of approved versions. No methodologically sound published defense of the categorical negation survives; no Phase-1 defense object exists (disputed skipped the defender).

sources.ref: Leo XIII, Officiorum ac Munerum (1897), vatican.va
sources.independence_note: The governing norm in 1911; primary text read within the standard history of the Index (Fragnito's framework).

sources.ref: Leo XIII, Gospel-reading indulgence (Dec 1898); Providentissimus Deus (1893); Pius X / Pia Società di San Girolamo
sources.independence_note: Papal acts genealogically distinct from the Index-law line; independently defeat the negation.

sources.ref: USCCB, 'Changes in Catholic Attitudes Toward Bible Readings'
sources.independence_note: Catholic institutional concession on practice rarity — supports the weakened version from the confessional side opposite to White's.

sources.ref: G. Fragnito, La Bibbia al rogo (1997) / Church, Censorship and Culture (Cambridge UP, 2001)
sources.independence_note: Academic anchor for the restrictive history behind the weakened version.

rulings.would_change_this: Documentary evidence that the 1897 permissions were revoked or a dead letter church-wide circa 1911 — e.g., Holy Office or diocesan instructions penalizing lay reading of approved versions — would move the as-written ruling back toward genuinely-open.
rulings.notes: Largest framing gap in the dossier (4): categorical negation dead, substantive picture (conditioned access, thin lay Bible culture) solid.

quantifier_audit: it is withheld from the common people (categorical present, 1911)

components.component: The Roman Church reserves to the clergy/magisterium the right to authoritatively explain Scripture ('ecclesiastics alone are competent').
components.rationale: Documented doctrine in force in 1911: Trent Sess. IV, Vatican I Dei Filius, the 1907 anti-modernist measures. The Church's own formulations reserve authentic interpretation to the magisterium.

components.component: The Bible 'is withheld from the common people' (1911 present tense).
components.rationale: The categorical present fails against the 1897-1911 regime (approved annotated versions licit; 1898 indulgence; Pius X's Gospel distribution). Unlike GC-35-017's flat 'not allowed', 'withheld' admits a partial/practical reading with real documented content: Protestant and unannotated versions were forbidden, the liturgy remained Latin, lay Bible reading stayed marginal, and in the Italian/Spanish orbit near-total withholding was recent memory (until 1757/1783). One decisive counter-set defeats the universal; the practical kernel keeps it at improbable rather than discredited.

components.component: The withholding is grounded on the ecclesiastics-alone competence claim (stated causal ground).
components.rationale: The historical rationale of Rule IV and Inter Praecipuas (1844) is indeed that unguided reading harms the unlearned and that interpretation belongs to the Church; as a characterization of the restriction's stated ground this is accurate, though by 1911 the same authority was simultaneously promoting guided reading.

rulings.weakened_version: The Roman Church reserved the interpretation of Scripture to the magisterium and permitted lay access only through approved annotated versions, forbidding all others, so that direct unmediated Bible reading remained restricted and uncommon among Catholic laity in 1911.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the as-written present tense combines the 19th-century papal condemnations of Bible societies (Gregory XVI, Inter Praecipuas, 1844, in a line from Pius VII through Pius IX) with the de facto rarity of lay Bibles in Catholic countries circa 1900 (conceded by USCCB institutional history) and the recentness of the Italian/Spanish relaxations. It keeps the weakened version at well-supported but fails for the categorical wording: the condemnations targeted unapproved versions read 'unguided' while restating the approved pathway, and by 1911 the papacy was actively distributing Gospels and indulgencing their reading — conduct incompatible with 'it is withheld from the common people' as a standing description. No Phase-1 defense object exists (disputed skipped the defender); the verifier's 'recurring pattern' rationale is confirmed with the dual ruling making the pattern explicit.

sources.ref: Gregory XVI, Inter Praecipuas (1844), papalencyclicals.net
sources.independence_note: Best documentary case FOR the claim's 19th-century background; read against its own approved-version clause.

sources.ref: Leo XIII, Officiorum ac Munerum (1897); 1898 indulgence; Pius X / Society of St. Jerome
sources.independence_note: The 1911-state documents; genealogically distinct from the 1844 condemnation line.

sources.ref: G. Fragnito, La Bibbia al rogo (1997)
sources.independence_note: Independent academic anchor for the pre-1757 withholding regime that gives the kernel its content.

sources.ref: Council of Trent Sess. IV; Vatican I Dei Filius
sources.independence_note: Doctrinal basis of component 1, independent of the Index-law line.

sources.ref: W. François / Regensburg Forum survey, 'Vernacular Scripture in the Reformation Era'
sources.independence_note: Leuven revisionist line; one genealogical unit with Gow.

rulings.would_change_this: A quantitative study showing approved vernacular Bibles effectively unavailable to Catholic laity across countries circa 1911, or curial instructions nullifying the 1897 permissions, would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open or probable.

quantifier_audit: Papal councils decreed (plural, as historical fact)

components.component: Papal councils and church authorities decreed that certain books and writings be burned.
components.rationale: Abundantly documented: Constance (1415) ordered Wycliffe's and Hus's books burned; Toulouse 1229 and Tarragona 1233/34 ordered surrender/burning of vernacular Scriptures; papal orders against the Talmud from 1239; the Clementine Observatio (1596) ordered unauthorized vernacular Bibles destroyed (Lea vol. 1 local, lines 18677-18749; Fragnito).

components.component: Those decrees targeted 'books and writings containing records of Rome's cruelty toward dissenters' — i.e., conciliar texts aimed at persecution-records as such.
components.rationale: No located council decree describes its targets this way; every documented decree states heresy, error, or blasphemy as its object. The verifier found none; two claim-specific searches found none; the claim's genealogical node is GC itself with 19th-century Protestant antecedents (the 'efface all the records of the ancient Waldenses' topos). A partially matching reading survives — the Index banned Protestant martyrologies (Foxe, Crespin), which literally record persecutions, and burned heretical corpora included the dissenters' own accounts — so improbable rather than discredited; but the effect of doctrinally framed decrees is not what the sentence asserts, which is decrees aimed at cruelty-records.

components.component: Rome's purpose was to destroy the evidence of her own cruelty ('endeavored to destroy every record of her cruelty').
components.rationale: No documentary evidence of an evidence-suppression motive; the competing mainstream account of the motive — suppression of heresy and blasphemy — is documented in the decrees' own texts (Constance, Toulouse, the Index rules, the 1319 Toulouse Talmud sentence's stated grounds in Bernard Gui's proceedings). Per charter §3, undocumented motive with a documented rival account caps at improbable.

rulings.weakened_version: Church authorities repeatedly decreed the destruction of dissenters' books and writings, with the effect that much of the dissenters' own record — including their accounts of persecution — perished, so their history survives largely in their persecutors' documents.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest defense reads the claim through its effects: the Tridentine Index banned Protestant martyrologies — books that are precisely 'records of Rome's cruelty toward dissenters' — and the burning of Waldensian, Lollard, and Hussite corpora destroyed persecution accounts along with doctrine; modern heresy scholarship (Cameron, Biller, Audisio on the Waldensians) agrees the movement's medieval history is known almost entirely through inquisitorial records, which is White's own adjacent sentence. The defense establishes the weakened version but not the sentence as written: attributing to conciliar decrees a target-description ('records of cruelty') that no known decree contains converts an effect into a documented intention. It fails on provenance, not on the confession of its carriers. No Phase-1 defense object exists (disputed, confidence low, skipped the defender).

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages, vol. 1 — local file, lines 18677-18749 (book-burning record and its stated doctrinal grounds)
sources.independence_note: Standard older academic reference, independent of the council texts it digests.

sources.ref: Council of Constance decrees (papalencyclicals.net, Tanner-based text)
sources.independence_note: Primary conciliar text; documents doctrinal grounds, no cruelty-records language.

sources.ref: G. Fragnito, La Bibbia al rogo (1997) — the 1596 destruction order
sources.independence_note: Independent archival line for the early-modern destruction decrees.

sources.ref: Musée protestant / Waldensian historiography survey (knowledge of Waldensians via Catholic records)
sources.independence_note: Supports the weakened version's commonplace; genealogically separate from Lea.

rulings.would_change_this: An actual conciliar or papal decree text — in Mansi, the Holy Office archive, or an inquisitorial register — ordering destruction of writings expressly because they record persecutions would flip the failing components and vindicate the sentence as written.
rulings.notes: source_quality academic-partial because the failing components rest on documented decree texts plus an absence-of-evidence finding within budget; modern academic literature engages the effect-commonplace, not White's specific decree assertion.
